--- Dr. Abdo Ismail ---
Dr. Abdo Ismail DDS | MS Dr. Ismail is a highly respected, board-certified periodontist with a passion for delivering exceptional care in periodontal and implant dentistry. Born in the picturesque city of Geneva, Switzerland, Dr. Ismails journey in dentistry has taken him across continents, driven by a deep commitment to clinical excellence and lifelong learning.

Many of our patients arrive feeling unsure, anxious, or overwhelmed—especially after a referral. That’s why our first priority isn’t treatment. It’s understanding your concerns and making sure you know exactly what to expect. What happens during your consultation. We listen first We take time to understand your referral, concerns, and goals. Clear explanations You’ll receive easy-to-understand information about your condition and options.

As a Diplomate of the American Board of Periodontology and a member of the American Academy of Periodontology (AAP), Dr. Ismail remains at the forefront of advancements in his field. His professional affiliations reflect his commitment to excellence and to staying current with the latest innovations in periodontal and implant care. Beyond his professional achievements, Dr. Ismail is a devoted family man who enjoys spending time with his wife and their two young daughters, Layal and Kenz.

Al Yafi has continuously advanced his skills and knowledge, completing specialized training in oral surgery and later earning a Master of Science degree in Periodontics from the University of Kentucky, one of the nation’s premier periodontal residency programs. Driven by a lifelong passion for excellence and innovation, Dr. Al Yafi has dedicated his career to mastering implantology, bone grafting, and advanced dentoalveolar surgery.

In his free time, he enjoys swimming, playing tennis, and attending car races and car shows. Contact Now

--- Dr. Firas Al Yafi ---
Dr. Firas Al Yafi DDS | MS Dr. Firas Al Yafi is a highly accomplished dentist and board-certified periodontist celebrated for his outstanding clinical expertise and pioneering contributions to modern dentistry. Since graduating from dental school in 2000, Dr.

As a co-founder of Prime Periodontics & Implant Center, Dr. Al Yafi remains at the forefront of dental care advancement across North Texas. His philosophy centers on combining artistry, technology, and compassion, ensuring that each patient receives personalized, state-of-the-art treatment designed for long-term health, function, and confidence.

His clinical focus encompasses all aspects of dental implant therapy, including computer-guided full-arch rehabilitation (All-on-X), complex bone regeneration, and aesthetic soft-tissue microsurgery for treating gum recession. A strong advocate for digital dentistry, Dr. Al Yafi is recognized for his deep expertise in Cone Beam Computed Tomography (CBCT) applications and computer-guided implant surgery, which enable him to achieve unparalleled precision, predictability, and patient comfort.

He is LANAP® and LAPIP® certified, offering minimally invasive laser periodontal therapy for the treatment of gum disease and peri-implantitis. In addition to his clinical pr actice, Dr. Al Yafi has authored and published research in the fields of implant surgery and regenerative procedures, contributing valuable insights to the scientific community. His work reflects a commitment not only to excellence in patient care but also to advancing the profession through innovation and education.

Contact Us Why Do Canines Become Impacted. Canines may become impacted due to several factors, such as: Insufficient Space in the Dental Arch: There may not be enough room for the canine to erupt due to crowding or improper alignment of other teeth.

From preserving natural teeth to restoring full confidence through state-of-the-art implant technology, we provide compassionate, customized care using the latest digital tools and guided surgical systems.

--- Exposure of Impacted Teeth ---
Exposure of Impacted Teeth for Orthodontic Treatment: Uncovering Hidden Smiles Impacted teeth, especially canines, can create complications during orthodontic treatment.

When a tooth is “impacted,” it means that it has not fully emerged from the gum line and is trapped in the jawbone or soft tissue, preventing it from erupting into its proper position. The most common impacted tooth, after wisdom teeth, is the canine. This tooth plays a critical role in guiding your bite and maintaining facial structure. When impacted canines do not erupt naturally, our specialists can assist by surgically exposing the tooth.

Consultation and Imaging The process begins with a thorough examination, often accompanied by X-rays or 3D scans, to assess the exact position of the impacted tooth. Our Specialists will coordinate with your orthodontist to plan the most effective treatment approach. Surgical Exposure Using local anesthesia to ensure your comfort, our specialists will make a small incision in the gum tissue to uncover the impacted tooth.

Early intervention ensures that your orthodontic treatment proceeds smoothly and that the impacted tooth can be brought into alignment with the rest of your smile. Addressing an impacted tooth in a timely manner can also reduce the risk of damage to adjacent teeth, root resorption, or the development of cysts around the impacted tooth. Is Exposure of Impacted Teeth Right for You.

Early detection and treatment can prevent these complications and improve the overall success of your orthodontic plan. The Exposure Procedure: How Our Specialists Help Impacted Teeth Erupt The process of exposing an impacted tooth is a minor surgical procedure designed to reveal the tooth and make it accessible for orthodontic movement. This procedure is often done in conjunction with braces or other orthodontic appliances to bring the impacted tooth into alignment.

Genetic Factors: Some people are more prone to thin gum tissue or gum recession due to genetics. Orthodontic Treatment: Teeth that have been moved through orthodontic treatment can experience gum recession, especially if the gum tissue is naturally thin. When the gumline recedes, it can expose the roots of your teeth, which are more vulnerable to decay and damage. This can also lead to increased sensitivity to hot and cold foods and beverages.

Misaligned Teeth or Abnormal Eruption Patterns: When adjacent teeth block the path of the canine, it can remain trapped beneath the gums. Genetic Factors: Some individuals are more prone to impacted teeth due to family history or developmental issues. Impacted canines that are left untreated can cause a variety of dental issues, including improper bite alignment, damage to nearby teeth, and even cyst formation.

Together, we’ll help uncover your smile, ensuring a healthy and beautiful result. --- Tissue Grafting ---
Tissue Grafting: Restoring Healthy Gums and Protecting Your Smile Tissue grafting is a periodontal procedure used to treat receding gums or areas with insufficient gum tissue. Receding gums expose the roots of your teeth, which can lead to increased sensitivity, a higher risk of decay, and potential tooth loss. Additionally, receding gums can affect the aesthetics of your smile.

Tissue grafting, also known as gum grafting, helps restore the gumline and protect the teeth, while improving both the function and appearance of your smile. Why Is Tissue Grafting Necessary. Gum recession can occur for various reasons, including: Periodontal Disease: Infection and inflammation of the gums can lead to tissue loss. Aggressive Brushing: Overbrushing or using a hard-bristled toothbrush can wear away gum tissue over time.

Healing and Orthodontic Movement After the exposure procedure, the gums will heal, and your orthodontist will gradually adjust the position of the impacted tooth, using gentle pressure to help it emerge into the correct alignment. The complete process can take several months, depending on the tooth’s position and how quickly it moves. Benefits of Early Treatment Exposing impacted teeth early, especially canines, can prevent more complex dental issues down the road.

If the tooth is covered by bone, a portion of the bone may be gently removed to expose the tooth. Attaching an Orthodontic Bracket Once the tooth is exposed, our specialists may attach a small orthodontic bracket and chain to the tooth. This chain is then connected to your braces or another orthodontic appliance. Over time, your orthodontist will use this attachment to carefully guide the tooth into its proper place within the dental arch.

If you or your child has been diagnosed with an impacted tooth during orthodontic treatment, surgical exposure may be the key to achieving a healthy, aligned smile. Working closely with your orthodontist, our specialists will determine the best course of action to ensure that the impacted tooth is successfully guided into position. Contact our specialists’ office in the DFW area to learn more about impacted tooth exposure and how it can benefit your orthodontic treatment.

Our specialists creates a small flap in the roof of your mouth (palate) and removes a piece of connective tissue from underneath. This tissue is then placed over the exposed roots and secured with sutures. The flap in the palate is stitched back to heal naturally. Free Gingival Graft This technique is often recommended for patients with naturally thin gums. A small piece of tissue is taken directly from the surface of the palate and grafted onto the affected area.

Benefits of Tissue Grafting Tissue grafting offers several important benefits for both your oral health and smile aesthetics: Prevents Further Gum Recession: By adding new tissue to the gumline, tissue grafting protects the teeth from further recession. Reduces Tooth Sensitivity: Covering exposed roots helps alleviate discomfort when consuming hot or cold foods and beverages.

Our specialists will evaluate your condition and recommend the best treatment plan to restore the health and appearance of your gums. Contact our specialists’ office in the DFW area today to schedule a consultation and learn more about how tissue grafting can protect your teeth and enhance your smile.

Tissue grafting restores the gumline, covering the exposed roots and protecting them from further damage. Types of Tissue Grafting Procedures Our specialists offer different types of tissue grafting techniques depending on the extent of gum recession and the patient’s individual needs. These include: Connective Tissue Graft This is the most common type of gum grafting procedure.

The Tissue Grafting Procedure: What to Expect Our specialists performs tissue grafting in a way that minimizes discomfort and promotes quick healing. The procedure is done under local anesthesia, though sedation options are available for patients who feel anxious. 1 Consultation and Evaluation During your initial consultation, our specialists will assess the extent of your gum recession and determine which type of grafting procedure is most appropriate.

3 Post-Operative Care After the procedure, it is essential to follow our specialist’s care instructions for optimal healing. You may experience some tenderness and swelling, which can be managed with over-the-counter pain medications or any prescription your doctor provides. You will be advised to avoid brushing or flossing around the grafted area until it fully heals, as well as: Rinsing with a prescribed mouthwash to prevent infection.

Protects Against Decay and Tooth Loss: Restoring healthy gum tissue protects the vulnerable roots from decay and damage, reducing the risk of future tooth loss. Improves Aesthetics: Tissue grafting can improve the appearance of your smile by evening out the gumline and covering any visible tooth roots. Is Tissue Grafting Right For You. If you have receding gums or are experiencing sensitivity due to exposed roots, tissue grafting may be the right solution for you.

This method is effective for both improving gum thickness and covering exposed roots. Pedicle Graft. In this procedure, the grafted tissue comes from the gum tissue near the affected tooth. Our specialists create a flap of tissue (called a pedicle) and gently pull it over the exposed root. This method works well when the patient still has sufficient healthy gum tissue near the area needing treatment.

A detailed plan will be created to ensure the best results. 2 Grafting Procedure On the day of the surgery, our specialists will numb the area to be treated and remove the necessary tissue for the graft. The tissue is then carefully positioned over the affected area and secured with small sutures. The procedure typically takes about an hour, depending on how many teeth are being treated.

Eating soft foods like yogurt, eggs, or pasta for a few days to avoid disturbing the graft. Avoiding hard or crunchy foods that could irritate the graft site. Most patients recover fully within two weeks, and follow-up appointments will allow our specialists to monitor your healing progress.

Extraction Sites: After a tooth extraction, PRF can be placed in the socket to promote healing, reduce the risk of dry socket, and accelerate the regeneration of soft tissue. Sinus Lifts: PRF is also used in sinus lift procedures to promote healing of the sinus membrane and encourage new bone formation in preparation for dental implants.

--- Platelet Rich Fibrin ---
Platelet-Rich Fibrin (PRF): Enhancing Healing and Regeneration Platelet-Rich Fibrin (PRF) is an advanced, natural technique used in dentistry and periodontics to promote faster healing and tissue regeneration. PRF is derived from a small sample of the patient’s own blood and contains a high concentration of growth factors, platelets, and healing proteins that significantly enhance the body’s ability to repair and regenerate tissue.

Uses of PRF in Periodontics and Implant Dentistry PRF is beneficial for a wide range of periodontal and implant procedures, including: Bone Grafting: PRF is often used in conjunction with bone grafting procedures to support the integration of grafted material and encourage new bone growth. This is particularly important for patients preparing for dental implants where adequate bone volume is needed.

Periodontal Surgery: During gum surgery, such as tissue grafting or flap procedures, PRF helps promote faster healing of the gum tissue and reduce post-operative discomfort and inflammation. Dental Implants: PRF is commonly applied in implant procedures to enhance osseointegration—the process by which the implant fuses with the surrounding bone. This results in a stronger, more stable foundation for the dental implant.

This cutting-edge approach has been widely adopted in various dental procedures, including bone grafting, periodontal surgery, and implant placement, to optimize recovery and improve outcomes. Contact Us What is PRF. PRF is a second-generation platelet concentrate that is 100% natural and autologous, meaning it is created from the patient’s own blood. It is a byproduct of centrifuging the blood sample to separate the components.

Benefits of PRF PRF offers several advantages for patients undergoing dental and periodontal procedures, including: Accelerated Healing: PRF provides a natural source of growth factors and proteins that speed up tissue repair and recovery. Reduced Inflammation: PRF contains anti-inflammatory proteins that help minimize swelling and discomfort after surgery. Lower Risk of Infection: The leukocytes in PRF help fight off infection, promoting a healthier post-operative healing environment.

Unlike its predecessor, Platelet-Rich Plasma (PRP), PRF does not require any additives or anticoagulants, making it a completely natural option for supporting healing. PRF is rich in: Platelets: Cells that help with blood clotting and wound healing. Leukocytes (white blood cells): Cells that are essential for fighting infection and inflammation. Fibrin: A protein that forms a scaffold to support tissue healing and regeneration.

It involves the following steps: Blood Collection: A small amount of the patient’s blood is drawn, similar to what would be collected for a routine blood test. Centrifugation: The blood sample is placed into a specialized centrifuge, which spins at a specific speed and duration. This process separates the blood into distinct layers, including red blood cells, platelets, and plasma. The PRF layer, rich in platelets and healing factors, is extracted.

Growth Factors: Special proteins that stimulate cell proliferation and tissue regeneration. When used in dental surgery, PRF acts as a biologic scaffold that accelerates tissue repair and regeneration while promoting better integration with the surrounding tissues. How is PRF Created and Applied. The process of obtaining PRF is simple and minimally invasive.

Application: Once the PRF is collected, it is immediately applied to the surgical site. In procedures like bone grafting, gum surgery, or implant placement, PRF enhances healing, promotes faster tissue regeneration, and reduces the risk of infection.

Completely Natural and Safe: Since PRF is derived from the patient’s own blood, there is no risk of allergic reactions or disease transmission. Improved Outcomes for Bone Grafting and Implant Placement: PRF enhances the body’s ability to regenerate bone and soft tissue, leading to better long-term results for dental implants and grafting procedures. Is PRF Right For You.

A frenectomy can help improve the long-term success of orthodontic treatment. The Frenectomy Procedure: What to Expect Our specialists perform frenectomies in a minimally invasive manner, ensuring patient comfort and a quick recovery. The procedure is typically done using local anesthesia, though sedation options are available for patients who may feel anxious.

Our specialists may use traditional surgical techniques or a laser, which can minimize bleeding and reduce healing time. The procedure is quick, often taking less than 30 minutes. Post-Operative Care and Healing: Following the procedure, most patients experience mild discomfort that can be managed with over-the-counter pain relievers. Swelling and soreness are common but usually subside within a few days.

Our specialists may recommend PRF as part of your treatment if you are undergoing dental implants, bone grafting, gum surgery, or tooth extractions. By integrating PRF into your care, you can expect enhanced healing, reduced risk of complications, and improved long-term outcomes for your oral health. If you’re interested in learning more about Platelet-Rich Fibrin and how it can benefit your treatment, contact our specialists’ office in the DFW area to schedule a consultation today.

Diastema (Gap Between Teeth) A prominent labial frenum can create or maintain a gap between the upper or lower front teeth, known as a diastema. In conjunction with orthodontic treatment, a frenectomy can help close this gap by allowing the teeth to move into proper alignment without interference. Orthodontic Stability When orthodontic treatment is planned or already underway, removing an abnormal frenum may be necessary to ensure that teeth stay in place after braces are removed.

Here are some common conditions that may require a frenectomy. Lip-Tie or Tongue-Tie (Ankyloglossia) When the lingual frenum is too short or tight, it restricts the movement of the tongue, a condition known as tongue-tie. This can interfere with speaking, eating, swallowing, and even breastfeeding in infants. A frenectomy can free the tongue, allowing for better mobility.

An abnormally positioned or overly large frenum can cause several oral health issues, including restricted tongue movement, gaps between teeth, gum recession, or difficulties with speech and feeding. Frenectomy procedures, performed by our specialists, can resolve these concerns and improve oral function and aesthetics. Contact Us Why Is a Frenectomy Necessary. A frenectomy is often recommended for both functional and orthodontic reasons.

Evaluation and Preparation: During your consultation, our specialists will assess the position of the frenum and determine if a frenectomy is the appropriate treatment. For patients undergoing orthodontic treatment, coordination with your orthodontist will ensure the best outcome. Surgical Removal of the Frenum: The frenectomy itself involves making a small incision to release or remove the frenum.

Gum Recession Due to a High Labial Frenum A labial frenum that is positioned too close to the gumline can pull on the gums, leading to recession and exposing the roots of the teeth. If left untreated, this can cause sensitivity, an increased risk of tooth decay, and aesthetic concerns. A frenectomy can relieve the tension on the gums and prevent further damage.

--- Frenectomy ---
Frenectomy: Correcting Abnormal Frenum Attachments for Improved Oral Health A frenectomy is a simple surgical procedure that removes or modifies the frenum, a small fold of tissue that connects the lips, cheeks, or tongue to the gums and underlying bone. There are two main types of frenums in the mouth: Labial Frenum: This connects the upper or lower lip to the gums between the front teeth. Lingual Frenum: This connects the underside of the tongue to the floor of the mouth.

--- Esthetic Crown Lengthening ---
Esthetic Crown Lengthening: Enhancing Your Smile Esthetic crown lengthening is a periodontal procedure designed to improve the appearance of your smile by reshaping the gum tissue to expose more of your teeth. This procedure is often recommended for patients who have a “gummy smile,” where an excess of gum tissue covers too much of the teeth, making them appear shorter than they actually are.

Once the excess gum tissue is removed, the gum line is contoured to reveal more of your teeth and create a balanced, natural appearance. The entire process is usually completed in a single visit and can significantly enhance the aesthetics of your smile.

Our specialists will provide detailed aftercare instructions to ensure proper healing, including: Gently cleaning the area with prescribed mouthwash or saltwater rinses Avoiding hard or crunchy foods for a few days Monitoring the area for signs of infection or complications Most patients can return to their normal activities within a day or two, and the treated area typically heals completely within a couple of weeks.

Benefits of Esthetic Crown Lengthening Improves the overall appearance of your smile by revealing more of your natural teeth Helps create a balanced gum-to-tooth ratio, enhancing facial aesthetics can be combined with other cosmetic treatments, like veneers or crowns, for a complete smile makeover Boosts confidence by creating a more attractive, natural smile If you’ve been self-conscious about the appearance of your smile or have been told you have a “gummy” smile, aesthetic crown lengthening can make a significant difference.

Our specialists perform esthetic crown lengthening to create a more balanced, attractive smile by adjusting the gum line. Why Consider Esthetic Crown Lengthening. If your teeth appear too short or your smile seems overly “gummy,” esthetic crown lengthening can help. This procedure not only enhances the proportions of your teeth but also improves your overall facial harmony.

Our specialists are committed to helping you achieve the beautiful, balanced smile you deserve. --- All-on-X ---
All-on-X For patients who have lost all teeth in either the upper or lower jaw, or both, full-arch dental implants offer a transformative solution. This treatment can be performed with fixed All-on-X bridges. Fixed All-on-X (All-on-4 or All-on-6) The All-on-X approach (commonly known as All-on-4 or All-on-6) is a fixed, non-removable full-arch restoration supported by dental implants.

Enhance Orthodontic Outcomes: Removing a restrictive frenum can aid in closing gaps between teeth and ensure that orthodontic treatment results are long-lasting. Boost Confidence: Correcting a lip or tongue-tie can improve your smile and overall oral appearance, giving you greater confidence in your daily interactions. Is a Frenectomy Right for You.

If you or your child has a lip-tie, tongue-tie, or an abnormally placed frenum that is affecting oral function or aesthetics, a frenectomy may be the solution. Our specialists will thoroughly evaluate your condition and recommend the best treatment plan to restore optimal oral health. Contact our specialists’ office in the DFW area today to schedule a consultation and learn more about how a frenectomy can improve your smile and overall oral function.

It’s commonly performed for aesthetic reasons but may also be part of a more comprehensive treatment plan if you’re receiving cosmetic restorations like veneers or crowns. The Procedure: What to Expect During esthetic crown lengthening, our specialists will gently reshape the gum tissue and, in some cases, remove a small amount of bone around the teeth to expose more of their natural surface. The procedure is typically performed under local anesthesia to ensure comfort throughout.

Benefits of a Frenectomy The benefits of a frenectomy are both functional and aesthetic. By addressing a problematic frenum, you can: Improve Oral Function: A frenectomy can alleviate speech difficulties, improve feeding or breastfeeding in infants, and enhance tongue mobility for better eating and swallowing. Prevent Gum Recession: Reducing tension on the gumline helps prevent further recession and protects the health of your teeth.

To learn more about dental implant solutions or to schedule a consultation, contact our specialists’ office in the DFW area. --- Bone Grafting ---
Bone Grafting Before Implant Placement Preparing for a Strong Foundation Permanent Tooth Replacement Solutions at Prime Periodontics For patients considering dental implants, a healthy and sufficient amount of jawbone is essential to support the implant properly.

A titanium post is surgically placed into the jawbone, providing a strong, stable foundation for a crown, bridge, or denture. Implants look, feel, and function like natural teeth, and they also help preserve jawbone health by stimulating bone tissue.

--- Dental Implants vs. Dentures: What’s the Right Choice for You. ---
When it comes to replacing missing teeth, two of the most common options are dental implants and dentures. But how do you know which one is right for you. At Prime Periodontics, we help patients in Prosper and across North Texas make informed choices that best fit their oral health needs and lifestyle. What Are Dentures. Dentures are removable appliances used to replace multiple missing teeth.

Key Differences: Feature Stability Comfort Bone Health Maintenance Lifespan Cost (initially) Dentures May shift or loosen May cause gum irritation Can lead to bone loss Daily removal & cleaning 5–8 years (typically) Lower upfront cost Dental Implants Secure and permanent Feels like natural teeth Preserves and stimulates bone Brushed like natural teeth 15–25+ years with proper care Higher upfront, lower long-term Which One Should You Choose.
